title: The Manchu Conquest
bullets: The Manchus were not Chinese: a Tungusic-speaking forest people from northeast of the Great Wall, in 1644 numbering perhaps a million, who would rule a fifth of humanity for 268 years.
Nurhaci built the Eight Banners, a system that made the entire society into an army, then proclaimed a revived Later Jin state in 1616 and went to war with the Seven Grievances in 1618.
His son Hong Taiji renamed the people Manchu in 1635 and the dynasty Qing in 1636, claiming the Mandate of Heaven and announcing in advance that he meant to replace the Ming.
The Ming dynasty fell to a Chinese peasant rebel, not the Manchus; the Ming general Wu Sangui opened Shanhai Pass and let the Manchu army through, the worst miscalculation in Chinese history.
The regent Dorgon ran the conquest in the name of the child Shunzhi Emperor, presenting the Qing as the avenger of the Ming and keeping the entire Ming bureaucracy intact.
The queue order of 1645 forced every Han man to shave his forehead and braid his hair on pain of death; when Yangzhou resisted, the Qing army massacred the population over ten days.
Ming loyalists fought on for nearly two decades as the Southern Ming; the warlord-pirate Koxinga expelled the Dutch from Taiwan in 1661, the first time Taiwan mattered to Chinese politics.
A million Manchus ruled over a hundred million Chinese through a dyarchy of paired Manchu and Han officials and walled banner garrisons, an arithmetic that worked brilliantly until the 19th century.

title: Kangxi: The Sixty-One-Year Reign
bullets: The Kangxi Emperor reigned 61 years, the longest in Chinese history, governing personally and in detail across six decades, an almost exact contemporary of Louis XIV of France.
At fifteen Kangxi removed the strongman regent Oboi using palace wrestlers he had trained under cover of court games, a story that sounds like legend and is basically true.
The Revolt of the Three Feudatories nearly split the empire for eight years; the conquest was only truly finished at the Battle of Penghu in 1683, when the Qing annexed Taiwan.
The Treaty of Nerchinsk in 1689, negotiated with Jesuit interpreters, was China's first treaty with a European power, fixing the Russian border on terms of formal equality.
Kangxi personally led armies onto the steppe against the Dzungar leader Galdan, beginning a 70-year struggle for Inner Asia that would consume three reigns.
Kangxi studied Euclid with the Jesuits and tolerated their religion until the Chinese Rites Controversy, when a papal ruling on ancestor rites curdled imperial curiosity into suspicion.
He out-Confucianized the Confucians: the Sacred Edict broadcast sixteen moral maxims to every village, and the Kangxi Dictionary standardized over 47,000 characters.
In 1712 Kangxi froze the head-tax quota permanently, a generous and popular promise that became a fiscal time bomb as the population it could not tax tripled.

title: Yongzheng and the Machinery of Autocracy
bullets: The Yongzheng Emperor took the throne in 1722 amid a poisoned brawl of a succession and rumors he had falsified the will; he reigned only thirteen years and may have been the most important of the three.
A workaholic autocrat who slept four hours a night, Yongzheng annotated thousands of documents in vermilion ink, sometimes with margin notes longer than the reports themselves.
The palace memorial system gave the throne its own secret intelligence network, and the Grand Council around 1729 concentrated power in the emperor more tightly than any early modern state.
His fiscal reform legalized the customary surcharge and used it to pay officials 'money to nourish honesty,' an early serious attempt to fight corruption by removing its motive.
He merged the head tax into the land tax, shifting the burden onto land rather than bodies, and abolished the hereditary debased legal status of several outcast groups.
When the schoolteacher Zeng Jing plotted rebellion on anti-Manchu grounds, Yongzheng published a book-length argument that virtue, not ethnicity, confers the Mandate of Heaven.
Yongzheng crushed the Khoshut prince Lobzang Danjin and brought the high grassland region of Qinghai under direct Qing rule, prefiguring his son's far larger conquests.
He formally banned Christianity in 1724, expelling missionaries to Macau, a striking divergence from the same era when Christianity spread aggressively through the Spanish Americas.

title: The Qianlong Zenith
bullets: The Qianlong Emperor reigned sixty years then ruled three more as retired emperor; his reign was the unambiguous peak, with China's share of the world economy around a quarter to a third.
The Ten Great Campaigns ended the 70-year Dzungar struggle in a near-total extermination historians describe as genocide, several hundred thousand people annihilated by massacre and smallpox.

The destruction of the Dzungars opened the conquest of Xinjiang by 1759, roughly doubling the empire and drawing the borders the People's Republic still claims, including the Tibet claim and the Golden Urn.
The Siku Quanshu gathered some 36,000 volumes of Chinese learning and was simultaneously a censorship sweep: the literary inquisition destroyed thousands of anti-Manchu works.
Cao Xueqin, from a ruined Manchu bannerman family, wrote Dream of the Red Chamber, China's supreme novel, printed in full in 1791 and the subject of an entire field called Redology.
The Jesuit painter Giuseppe Castiglione set the court style, and the garden palaces northwest of Beijing reached their height, the most famous being the Yuanmingyuan.
Qianlong let the handsome, monumentally corrupt guardsman Heshen plunder the state for over two decades, amassing possibly the largest fortune from corruption in history.
In 1793 the Macartney mission was rebuffed with a letter to George III declaring China had no need of Britain's goods, the most consequential diplomatic misreading in modern history.

title: The Qing World System
bullets: The early modern global economy was largely a machine for moving American silver to China, paying for the tea, silk, and porcelain only China made well, with the Qing collecting the receipts.
Silver dug by forced labor at Potosí crossed the Pacific via Manila to buy tea bound for London and porcelain for European cabinets, making China the great economic sink of the age.
The Canton System after 1757 funneled all Western trade through one port and the licensed Cohong merchants, one of whom, Howqua, may have been the richest private individual on Earth.
China's population roughly tripled past 300 million toward 400 million in a century, close to a third of all humans alive, fed by New World crops that grew on useless hill soil.
The Columbian exchange that depopulated the Americas fed the Qing boom, but by 1800 it meant too many people, shrinking plots, and a state whose tax revenue Kangxi had frozen.
The salt monopoly created Yangzhou merchant dynasties so rich they bankrolled gardens, scholarship, and the Eccentrics of Yangzhou, one of China's first commercial art markets.
The Grand Canal moved millions of tons of tribute grain north to feed Beijing; pressure on the southeast coast pushed traders into the overseas Chinese diaspora the Qing officially disowned.
The world Macartney failed to understand was not a stagnant museum but a gigantic commercializing economy sitting, unknowing, directly in the path of the Industrial Revolution.

title: Cracks in the Mandate
bullets: The White Lotus Rebellion broke out in 1796, the year Qianlong stepped down, a millenarian revolt drawn from the landless migrants the population boom had created.
Suppressing it revealed the Eight Banners were militarily rotten, the dynasty had to rely on local militias, and Qianlong's financial reserves were gone.
The Jiaqing Emperor opened his reign by forcing the disgraced Heshen to suicide and seizing his world-historic fortune, but could not reverse a systemic decline.
In 1813 Eight Trigrams rebels fought their way inside the Forbidden City itself, so shocking that Jiaqing issued an edict of self-blame admitting the Mandate was in question.
Britain found the one commodity that reversed the silver flow: opium grown in British India, the same East India Company that taxed Bengal growing the poppy that broke China.
The silver drain doubled peasants' real tax burden without any tax being passed, turning a drug problem into the fiscal and agrarian crisis that radicalized the countryside.
The Daoguang Emperor faced a policy debate at court between legalizing opium and suppressing it, and chose suppression.
He sent the famously incorruptible Lin Zexu to Canton with full powers, a man completely right about the harm and completely wrong about what would happen next.

title: The First Opium War
bullets: Lin Zexu blockaded the factories and destroyed roughly 1,000 tons of opium in 1839; when Charles Elliot made the traders surrender it as Crown property, he handed Britain its pretext.
The First Opium War was one of the most morally unambiguous wars in modern history: Britain fought to force China to keep accepting a destructive narcotic, dressed as free trade.
The iron steam gunboat Nemesis could sail against wind and current up China's rivers and destroy wooden war junks at ranges from which they could not reply.
The Treaty of Nanking in 1842 ceded Hong Kong, opened five treaty ports, and imposed a large indemnity including reimbursement for the destroyed opium.
The Treaty of the Bogue added extraterritoriality and most-favored-nation status, a ratchet that meant every future concession to anyone instantly spread to everyone.

China was not colonized and kept its government, but lost control of its coast, its tariffs, and its legal jurisdiction over foreigners, and the psychological rupture was larger than the material one.
The scholar Wei Yuan urged China to learn the superior techniques of the barbarians to control them, and was almost entirely ignored for twenty years.

The century of humiliation is conventionally dated from here; the phrase itself is a later nationalist construction, but the resentment it crystallized is among the most powerful forces in the world today.

title: The Taiping Cataclysm
bullets: The Taiping Rebellion was the bloodiest civil war in human history before the 20th century, killing 20 to 30 million, at least twenty-five times the American Civil War running partly concurrently.
Hong Xiuquan, a village schoolteacher who failed the civil service examinations four times, decided after reading a Christian tract that he was the younger brother of Jesus Christ.
His God Worshipping Society grew among the marginalized Hakka of the southwestern hills, aiming not to replace a dynasty but to destroy Confucius, the gentry, and the Manchus together.
The Heavenly Kingdom took Nanjing in 1853 and ruled a third of China's richest land for a decade, a theocracy that banned opium, tobacco, alcohol, and foot-binding.
In 1856 the Taiping leadership destroyed itself in the Tianjing Incident, a paranoid bloodletting that gutted a leadership the movement never replaced.
The banner armies could not beat the Taiping; the dynasty was saved by the Confucian official Zeng Guofan, who raised the personally loyal, provincially funded Xiang Army.
His protégé Li Hongzhang raised the Huai Army on the same model; helped at the margins by Charles Gordon's Ever Victorious Army, they strangled the Taiping until Nanjing fell in 1864.
The Manchu monopoly on force broke permanently: real military and fiscal power now sat with provincial Han strongmen, and the dynasty's last fifty years balanced on top of them.

title: The Second Opium War and the Tongzhi Restoration
bullets: While the Taiping war raged, Britain and France used the Arrow Incident of 1856 to launch the Second Opium War and march an expeditionary force on Beijing itself.
In 1860, on Lord Elgin's orders, the army looted and burned the Yuanmingyuan, the act that more than any treaty clause lodged in Chinese memory as the symbol of the century.
The Treaties of Tianjin opened eleven more ports and legalized opium and missionaries, while Russia took over 600,000 square miles north of the Amur without firing a shot, founding Vladivostok.
The Xianfeng Emperor fled to Chengde and died there in 1861; his concubine, who had borne the only son, allied with princes in the Xinyou Coup and destroyed the regents.
Her name in history is Cixi, the Empress Dowager, the central political figure in China for 47 years across three child-emperors she installed and controlled.
Her reputation is contested: the older portrait of a vain reactionary despot is now argued to be partly the invention of a hostile reformer's propaganda.
The Tongzhi Restoration rebuilt the devastated provinces and created China's first foreign ministry, the Zongli Yamen, an admission the old tributary worldview was dead.
Vast separate Muslim revolts killed millions more and nearly tore Xinjiang away until Zuo Zongtang's brutal campaign reconquered it.

title: Self-Strengthening and Its Limits
bullets: The Self-Strengthening Movement ran for three decades under capable men and failed; why it failed is the most important question in this book.
Li Hongzhang and allies built the Jiangnan Arsenal, the Fuzhou Navy Yard, a steamship company, the Tongwen Guan, and the Beiyang Fleet, modernizing in the same decades as Japan.
Japan, after 1868, rebuilt its entire state on Western lines; China tried to bolt Western hardware onto an unreformed Confucian order, the experiment that still divides the two countries.
The slogan was Zhang Zhidong's: Chinese learning as the base, Western learning for use, the belief you could buy the gun without changing the society that fired it.
Failure was political, not technical: no central direction, regional rivalry, official corruption, and gentry obstruction that recalled the educational mission and tore up railways.
Navy funds were diverted to rebuild Cixi's marble Summer Palace, including a marble boat, the joke being it was the only ship the navy money actually bought.
The Sino-French War of 1884 stripped Vietnam off China and annihilated the Fuzhou fleet in a single afternoon, a warning filed as an ambiguous draw.
The real exam was ten years away, and China walked into it having learned almost nothing.

title: The Catastrophe: Japan, Concessions, the Hundred Days
bullets: Almost everyone expected China to win the First Sino-Japanese War in 1894; China was annihilated in months, the Beiyang Fleet wrecked at the Yalu and destroyed at Weihaiwei.
The shock was that China had been crushed by Japan, a neighbor it had regarded for two thousand years as a student, using the very model China had declined to adopt.
The Treaty of Shimonoseki ceded Taiwan to Japan, handed Korea to Japanese influence, and imposed an indemnity equal to multiple years of state revenue financed by foreign loans.
The Triple Intervention forced Japan to return the Liaodong Peninsula, then the powers collected their own price in the Scramble for Concessions and spheres of influence.
For a few years around 1898 diplomats genuinely expected China to be partitioned outright like Africa; mutual jealousy and the American Open Door notes helped prevent it.
Kang Youwei reread Confucius as a radical reformer to argue for constitutional monarchy, while Yan Fu's translations framed reform as a Darwinian struggle against national extinction.
In 1898 the Guangxu Emperor issued a torrent of radical edicts, the Hundred Days' Reform, which lasted 103 days before Cixi's counter-coup put him under lifelong palace arrest.
Six reformers, the Six Gentlemen, were beheaded; Tan Sitong deliberately refused to flee so the movement would have its martyrs, teaching a younger generation the dynasty could only be removed.

title: The Boxer Rising and the Last Reforms
bullets: Out of the lost war, the scramble, the failed reform, and a savage drought rose the Boxers, village militias who believed their rituals conferred invulnerability to bullets.
The Cixi-led court backed them and effectively declared war on all the foreign powers at once while Boxers besieged the Legation Quarter in Beijing for 55 days.
The dynasty's ablest southern governors flatly refused the war declaration and signed the Mutual Protection of the Southeast, the clearest measure of how little central authority remained.
The Eight-Nation Alliance stormed and looted Beijing; the Boxer Protocol of 1901 imposed a 450-million-tael indemnity, one for every Chinese subject, payable over four decades.
Russia occupied Manchuria, setting up the Russo-Japanese War of 1904, two foreign empires fighting on Chinese soil while the Qing declared neutrality in its own country.
After 1901 Cixi's court enacted the very reforms it had killed Guangxu's reformers for proposing, the New Policies: modern schools, a new army, a promised parliament.
In 1905 it abolished the civil service examination system that had bound the gentry to the throne for thirteen centuries, cutting the last cord and sending the brightest abroad as revolutionaries.
In 1908 the imprisoned Guangxu died one day before Cixi; a 2008 forensic examination found a fatal dose of arsenic, making deliberate poisoning the strongly supported conclusion.

title: The Xinhai Revolution and the End
bullets: The last emperor, Puyi, was enthroned at two in 1908 under his timid father Prince Chun, presiding over a court out of competence, money, time, and consent.
The New Policies created the very constituencies that buried the dynasty: a nationalist Japan-educated officer corps, insulted provincial assemblies, and revolutionary networks abroad.
Sun Yat-sen knit the revolutionary groups into the Tongmenghui around his Three Principles of the People; the feminist poet Qiu Jin was executed in 1907 plotting rebellion.
The Railway Protection Movement over a nationalization scheme drew troops away from Wuhan, thinning the garrison at exactly the wrong place.
An accidental bomb forced the Wuchang Uprising on 10 October 1911, Double Ten, after which province after province simply declared independence in the Xinhai Revolution.
The court turned to Yuan Shikai and his Beiyang Army; he negotiated with both sides and brokered the abdication in exchange for the presidency of the new republic.
On 12 February 1912 the regent issued the abdication edict for the six-year-old Puyi, ending the Qing and an imperial institution running back over two thousand years to the first emperor.

The Qing left the map the People's Republic still claims from the borders Qianlong's armies drew, and the century of humiliation grievance that is among the most powerful emotions in modern geopolitics.
